figures.caption: Figure 4: Convergence traces for representative n=32 instances. Top panels: holdout ε̂H (solid) and reported ε (dashed), with τb below. Bottom: credit heatmaps at the first, second, and last iterates.

Figure 8: Egalitarian effects on Synthetic n=32: non-egalitarian (left) vs. egalitarian (right). The QP objective redistributes credit more broadly, as visible in the heatmaps, with only minor impact on ε̂H relative to YP’s baseline.
Figure 9: Egalitarian effects on Synthetic n=32 (continued): non-egalitarian (left) vs. egalitarian (right). Credit heatmaps are visibly less sparse under the egalitarian objective.
Figure 12: Synthetic n=10, non-egalitarian convergence traces (cont.).

Figure 5: Credit assignment results on Synthetic (n=32) for non-egalitarian (dark) and egalitarian (light) settings. Lower ε̂H is better. Bars show 95% bootstrap CIs; points are individual problem instances. Compare with fig. 3 for BrowseComp-Plus.
Figure 15: BrowseComp n=10, non-egalitarian convergence traces (cont.).
Figure 16: BrowseComp n=10, egalitarian convergence traces. Heatmaps show broader credit redistribution; CG(R,·) methods match or undershoot YP’s baseline.
Figure 6: ZS failure cases: ZS overshoots YP’s holdout ε̂H by +0.23, while CG(R,R) matches the baseline with 3–4× fewer calls.
Figure 11: Credit assignment results on Synthetic (n=10) for non-egalitarian (dark) and egalitarian (light) settings. Lower ε̂H is better.
Figure 1: Convergence at tractable scale: Synthetic, n=10 (egal.). CG matches ε̂H in ∼30 calls.
Figure 17: Calls-to-target CDF at tolerance 0.01: fraction of n=10 instances within 0.01 of ε̂∗H as a function of LLM calls.
Figure 14: Credit assignment results on BrowseComp-Plus (n=10) for non-egalitarian (dark) and egalitarian (light) settings. Lower ε̂H is better.
Figure 10: Additional BrowseComp n=32 convergence traces. CG(R,R) consistently matches YP’s holdout ε̂H with 8–9× fewer calls; in Row 31 the LLM-oracle variants undershoot YP by 0.08.
Figure 7: ZS success cases: ZS matches YP’s holdout ε̂H = 0.50 in a single call, with high τb.

Figure 3: Credit assignment results on BrowseComp-Plus (n=32) for non-egalitarian (dark) and egalitarian (light) settings. Bars show 95% bootstrap CIs of the sample means; points are individual problem instances. See Figure 5 for the analogous Synthetic results.
Figure 13: Synthetic n=10, egalitarian convergence traces. Heatmaps show broader credit distribution with minimal ε̂H degradation relative to YP.
